docs.title: Rux Standard Library
docs.text: The official reference documentation for the Rux Standard Library.

docs.title: Modules
docs.text:
• Types
  • Text
  • IO
  • Utilities
  • Memory
  • System

docs.title: ReadLine
docs.text:
Reads a line of text from standard input.

Module: Std::Io

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: ReadLine() -> String

docs.title: Description
docs.text:
ReadLine reads a line of UTF-8 text from standard input and returns it as a String.

Trailing line terminators are automatically removed before the result is returned.

If an error occurs or end-of-file (EOF) is reached, an empty string is returned.

docs.title: ReadLine
docs.text:
ReadLine() -> String

Reads a line from standard input.

docs.title: Return Value
docs.text:
Returns a String containing the characters read from standard input.

Returns an empty string when:

  • End-of-file (EOF) is reached.
  • An input error occurs.
  • The input line contains only a line terminator.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
import Std::Io;

func Main() {

    Print("Enter your name: ");

    let name = ReadLine();

    PrintLine(
        "Hello, {}!",
        name
    );
}

Input:

Alex

Output:

Enter your name: Hello, Alex!

docs.title: Line Terminators
docs.text:
ReadLine() automatically removes trailing line terminators.

The following sequences are recognized:

\n
\r\n

Example:

Input:

Hello World\n

Returned string:

Hello World

docs.title: Empty Input
docs.text:
If the user presses Enter without typing any characters:

Input:

Returned value:

String::New()

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
let text = ReadLine();

if text.IsEmpty() {
    PrintLine("No input");
}

docs.title: Memory Management
docs.text:
The returned string owns its character buffer.

Memory is dynamically allocated before the string is returned.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
let text = ReadLine();

The returned string remains valid after the function returns.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Input is read as UTF-8 text.
  • Line terminators are removed automatically.
  • A new string is allocated for every successful call.
  • An empty string may indicate EOF, an input error, or an empty line.

docs.title: Linux
docs.text:
• Input is read from standard input using operating system read calls.
  • UTF-8 input is returned directly.

docs.title: Windows
docs.text:
• Input is read from the standard input handle using the Windows file API.
  • UTF-8 input is returned directly.

docs.title: Current Limitations
docs.text:
• Maximum input length is currently 4096 bytes.
  • Input longer than 4096 bytes is truncated.
  • BSD support is not yet implemented.
  • Illumos support is not yet implemented.
  • macOS support is not yet implemented.

These platforms currently contain placeholder implementations.

docs.title: Example: Simple Prompt
docs.text:
import Std::Io;

func Main() {

    Print("Username: ");
    let username = ReadLine();

    Print("Password: ");
    let password = ReadLine();

    PrintLine(
        "Welcome, {}!",
        username
    );
}

docs.title: IO
docs.text:
Console output utilities.

Module: Std::Io

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
Print(...)
PrintLine(...)

docs.title: Description
docs.text:
The Std::Io module provides console output functionality for strings, primitive types, and formatted text.

All supported primitive types can be printed directly. Internally, values are converted to strings before being written to the console.

The module supports:

  • Primitive numeric types
  • Boolean types
  • Character types
  • Strings
  • Formatted output using Display

docs.title: Print
docs.text: Writes a value to standard output without appending a newline.

docs.title: Primitive Types
docs.text:
Print(value: int8)
Print(value: int16)
Print(value: int32)
Print(value: int64)

Print(value: uint8)
Print(value: uint16)
Print(value: uint32)
Print(value: uint64)

Print(value: float32)
Print(value: float64)

Print(value: bool8)
Print(value: bool16)
Print(value: bool32)
Print(value: bool)

Print(value: char8)
Print(value: char16)
Print(value: char32)
Print(value: char)

docs.title: Strings
docs.text:
Print(string: *const char8, length: uint)
Print(string: *const char16, length: uint)

Print(string: char8[])
Print(string: char16[])

Print(string: String)

docs.title: Formatted Output
docs.text:
Print(
    format: char8[],
    args: Display...
)

Formats the arguments using Format() and writes the result to standard output.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
Print(
    "Hello, {}!",
    String::From("Alex")
);

Output:

Hello, Alex!

docs.title: PrintLine
docs.text: Writes a value to standard output and appends a newline.

docs.title: Newline Only
docs.text:
PrintLine()

Writes a line terminator.

docs.title: Primitive Types
docs.text:
PrintLine(value: int8)
PrintLine(value: int16)
PrintLine(value: int32)
PrintLine(value: int64)

PrintLine(value: uint8)
PrintLine(value: uint16)
PrintLine(value: uint32)
PrintLine(value: uint64)

PrintLine(value: float32)
PrintLine(value: float64)

PrintLine(value: bool8)
PrintLine(value: bool16)
PrintLine(value: bool32)
PrintLine(value: bool)

PrintLine(value: char8)
PrintLine(value: char16)
PrintLine(value: char32)
PrintLine(value: char)

docs.title: Strings
docs.text:
PrintLine(string: *const char8, length: uint)
PrintLine(string: *const char16, length: uint)

PrintLine(string: char8[])
PrintLine(string: char16[])

PrintLine(string: String)

docs.title: Formatted Output
docs.text:
PrintLine(
    format: char8[],
    args: Display...
)

Formats the arguments using Format(), writes the result, and appends a newline.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
PrintLine(
    "{} scored {} points",
    playerName,
    score
);

Output:

Alex scored 42 points

docs.title: Printing Strings
docs.text:
Print("Hello");
Print(" ");
Print("World");

Output:

Hello World

docs.title: Printing With Newlines
docs.text:
PrintLine("Hello");
PrintLine("World");

Output:

Hello
World

docs.title: Printing Numbers
docs.text:
PrintLine(42);
PrintLine(3.14);

Output:

42
3.14

docs.title: Printing Booleans
docs.text:
PrintLine(true);
PrintLine(false);

Output:

true
false

docs.title: Formatted Output
docs.text:
let name = String::From("Alex");

PrintLine(
    "Hello, {}!",
    name
);

Output:

Hello, Alex!

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Primitive values are converted to strings automatically.
  • Formatted output uses Format().
  • Values supplied to formatted output must implement Display.
  • Print() does not append a newline.
  • PrintLine() always appends a newline.

docs.title: Linux
docs.text:
• UTF-8 text is written directly to standard output.
  • Output is performed using operating system write calls.

docs.title: Windows
docs.text:
• UTF-8 text is converted to UTF-16 before being written to the console.
  • UTF-16 output is written using the Windows console API.

docs.title: Output Length Limit
docs.text:
Strings longer than 4096 characters currently cause a fatal error when printed. Attempting to print a longer UTF-8 string will terminate the program with:

Fatal("string is too long to print")

This limitation applies to:

Print(...)
PrintLine(...)

when the underlying string exceeds 4096 characters.

docs.title: Current Limitations
docs.text:
• BSD support is not yet implemented.
  • Illumos support is not yet implemented.
  • macOS support is not yet implemented.

These platforms currently contain placeholder implementations.

docs.title: Memory
docs.text:
Low-level memory management and memory manipulation utilities.

Module: Std::Memory

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
Alloc(size: uint) -> *opaque
Realloc(ptr: *opaque, size: uint) -> *opaque
Free(ptr: *opaque)

Set(ptr: *opaque, size: uint, value: int32)
Zero(ptr: *opaque, size: uint)

Compare(
    lhs: *const opaque,
    rhs: *const opaque,
    length: uint
) -> uint

Copy(
    dest: *opaque,
    src: *const opaque,
    length: uint
)

docs.title: Description
docs.text:
The Std::Memory module provides low-level memory allocation and manipulation functionality.

These functions operate on raw memory and are intended for implementing containers, strings, allocators, and other systems-level components.

Improper use can result in memory corruption, leaks, or undefined behavior.

docs.title: Alloc
docs.text:
Alloc(size: uint) -> *opaque

Allocates a block of memory.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Description size Number of bytes to allocate.

docs.title: Return Value
docs.text:
Returns a pointer to the allocated memory block.

Returns null if allocation fails.

docs.title: Example
docs.text: let ptr = Alloc(256);

docs.title: Realloc
docs.text:
Realloc(ptr: *opaque, size: uint) -> *opaque

Resizes a previously allocated memory block.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Description ptr Existing memory block. size New size in bytes.

docs.title: Return Value
docs.text: Returns a pointer to the resized memory block.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Existing contents are preserved up to the smaller of the old and new sizes.
  • Passing null behaves like Alloc().
  • Passing 0 frees the memory and returns null.

docs.title: Example
docs.text: ptr = Realloc(ptr, 512);

docs.title: Free
docs.text:
Free(ptr: *opaque)

Releases memory previously allocated by Alloc() or Realloc().

docs.title: Example
docs.text: Free(ptr);

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Passing null is allowed.
  • Accessing memory after it has been freed is undefined behavior.

docs.title: Set
docs.text:
Set(
    ptr: *opaque,
    size: uint,
    value: int32
)

Fills a block of memory with the specified byte value.

docs.title: Example
docs.text: Set(buffer, 128, 0xFF);

docs.title: Zero
docs.text:
Zero(
    ptr: *opaque,
    size: uint
)

Fills a block of memory with zeros.

docs.title: Example
docs.text: Zero(buffer, 128);

docs.title: Compare
docs.text:
Compare(
    lhs: *const opaque,
    rhs: *const opaque,
    length: uint
) -> uint

Compares two memory regions.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Description lhs First memory block. rhs Second memory block. length Number of bytes to compare.

docs.title: Return Value
docs.text: Returns a platform-dependent comparison result.

docs.title: Platform Differences
docs.text: Current implementations do not return the same value on all platforms.

docs.title: Linux
docs.text:
Returns:

  • The index of the first differing byte.
  • length if all bytes are equal.

Examples:

Equal memory:
Compare(...) == length

First mismatch at byte 5:
Compare(...) == 5

docs.title: Windows
docs.text:
Returns:

  • The number of matching bytes.

Examples:

Equal memory:
Compare(...) == length

First mismatch at byte 5:
Compare(...) == 5

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
let result = Compare(
    bufferA,
    bufferB,
    64
);

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
Use this function only to determine whether memory regions differ, not to interpret ordering semantics.

Because the return values have different meanings across platforms, portable code should only rely on the following guarantee:

Compare(lhs, rhs, length) == length

This indicates that all compared bytes are equal.

Code should not rely on any other specific return value.

docs.title: Copy
docs.text:
Copy(
    dest: *opaque,
    src: *const opaque,
    length: uint
)

Copies bytes from one memory location to another.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Description dest Destination memory block. src Source memory block. length Number of bytes to copy.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
Copy(
    destination,
    source,
    256
);

docs.title: Notes
docs.text: Source and destination memory regions must be valid for at least length bytes.

docs.title: Allocating an Integer Array
docs.text:
import Std::Memory;

func Main() {

    let ptr = Alloc(
        10 * sizeof<int32>
    ) as *int32;

    Zero(
        ptr,
        10 * sizeof<int32>
    );

    Free(ptr);
}

docs.title: Windows
docs.text: Memory allocation is implemented using the process heap.

docs.title: Linux
docs.text: Memory allocation is implemented using anonymous memory mappings.

docs.title: Safety
docs.text:
The functions in this module operate on raw pointers.

The caller is responsible for:

  • Freeing allocated memory.
  • Avoiding buffer overflows.
  • Avoiding use-after-free bugs.
  • Ensuring valid pointer alignment where required.
  • Ensuring copied regions are valid.

Incorrect usage may result in crashes or undefined behavior.

docs.title: Current Limitations
docs.text:
• BSD support is not yet implemented.
  • Illumos support is not yet implemented.
  • macOS support is not yet implemented.

These platforms currently contain placeholder implementations.

docs.title: System
docs.text:
Low-level operating system functionality.

Module: Std::System

docs.title: Import
docs.text: import Std::System;

docs.title: Description
docs.text:
The Std::System module provides access to low-level operating system functionality.

Functions within this module interact directly with the underlying platform and are intended for tasks that require operating system integration. Implementations may vary between supported platforms such as Linux and Windows.

Many functions in this module expose behavior that affects the entire process, including process termination and other system-level operations.

docs.title: Platform Support
docs.text:
The Std::System module provides platform-specific implementations where necessary.

Platform Supported Linux yes Windows yes macOS Planned BSD Planned Illumos Planned

docs.title: Functions
docs.text: Function Description Exit Terminates the current process with a specified exit code.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Functions in this module may have platform-specific behavior.
  • Some operations may immediately terminate the current process.
  • Additional system-related functionality may be added in future releases of the standard library.

docs.title: Assert
docs.text: Terminates the program if a condition evaluates to false.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
Assert(
    condition: bool,
    message: char8[],
    file: char8[] = #file,
    function: char8[] = #function,
    line: uint = #line,
    column: uint = #column
)

docs.title: Description
docs.text:
Assert verifies that a condition is true.

If the condition evaluates to false, an error message is printed containing:

  • The assertion message
  • Source file
  • Line number
  • Column number
  • Function name

After printing the diagnostic information, the program exits with status code 2.

This function is intended for validating assumptions and detecting programming errors during development.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Description condition Expression that must evaluate to true. message Message displayed when the assertion fails. file Source file where the assertion occurred. Automatically provided by #file. function Function where the assertion occurred. Automatically provided by #function. line Source line where the assertion occurred. Automatically provided by #line. column Source column where the assertion occurred. Automatically provided by #column.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
import Std::Assert;
import Std::Io::Print;

func Main() {
    let value = 42;

    Assert(value > 0, "value must be positive");

    Print("Assertion passed");
}

Output:

Assertion passed

docs.title: Failed Assertion Example
docs.text:
import Std::Assert;
import Std::Io::Print;

func Main() {
    let value = -1;

    Assert(value > 0, "value must be positive");
}

Output:

Assertion failed: value must be positive
--> Main.rux:7:11
    in function Main

docs.title: Exit Code
docs.text: Code Meaning 2 Assertion failed

docs.title: Exit
docs.text:
Terminates the current process with the specified exit code.

Module: Std::System

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: import Std::System;

docs.title: Synopsis
docs.text: Exit(code: uint32)

docs.title: Description
docs.text:
Exit immediately terminates the current process and returns the specified exit code to the operating system.

Execution does not continue after a call to Exit.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Description code Exit code returned to the operating system.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
import Std::System;

func Main() {
    Exit(0);
}

docs.title: Exit Codes
docs.text:
The meaning of exit codes is application-defined.

By convention:

Code Meaning 0 Successful execution Non-zero An error occurred

docs.title: Fatal
docs.text: Terminates the program immediately with a fatal error message.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
Fatal(
    message: char8[],
    file: char8[] = #file,
    function: char8[] = #function,
    line: uint = #line,
    column: uint = #column
)

docs.title: Description
docs.text:
Fatal reports an unrecoverable error and immediately terminates program execution.

The function prints a diagnostic message containing:

  • The supplied error message
  • Source file
  • Line number
  • Column number
  • Function name

After printing the diagnostic information, the program exits with status code 1.

Unlike Assert, Fatal always terminates the program and does not require a condition.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Description message Description of the fatal error. file Source file where the error occurred. Automatically provided by #file. function Function where the error occurred. Automatically provided by #function. line Source line where the error occurred. Automatically provided by #line. column Source column where the error occurred. Automatically provided by #column.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
import Std::Fatal;

func Main() {
    Fatal("Configuration file is missing");
}

Output:

Fatal error: Configuration file is missing
--> Main.rux:4:10
    in function Main

docs.title: Exit Code
docs.text: Code Meaning 1 Fatal error

docs.title: Display
docs.text:
Interface for types that can be converted to a string representation.

Module: Std

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
interface Display {
    func ToString() -> String;
}

docs.title: Description
docs.text:
Display defines a common interface for converting values into a String.

Types implementing Display provide a ToString method that returns a string representation of the value.

This interface is commonly used by formatting, logging, and output functions that need a textual representation of arbitrary types.

docs.title: ToString
docs.text:
ToString() -> String

Returns a string representation of the current value.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
struct Person {
    name: String;
}

extend Person : Display {
    func ToString(self) -> String {
        return self.name;
    }
}

docs.title: Format
docs.text:
Formats values into a string using replacement fields.

Module: Std

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Format(fmt: char8[], args: Display...) -> String

docs.title: Description
docs.text:
Format creates a new string by replacing placeholder fields in a format string with the string representation of supplied arguments.

Arguments must implement the Display interface.

Each occurrence of {} is replaced by the next argument in the argument list.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Description fmt Format string containing zero or more {} placeholders. args Values used to replace placeholders.

docs.title: Return Value
docs.text: Returns a newly allocated String containing the formatted result.

docs.title: Placeholders
docs.text:
The following placeholder is supported:

{}

Each placeholder consumes one argument.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
let text = Format("Hello, {}!", name);

Result:

Hello, Alex!

docs.title: Single Argument
docs.text:
let name = String::From("Alex");

let text = Format("Hello, {}!", name);

Result:

Hello, Alex!

docs.title: Multiple Arguments
docs.text:
let text = Format(
    "{} scored {} points",
    playerName,
    score
);

Result:

Alex scored 42 points

docs.title: No Placeholders
docs.text:
let text = Format("Hello World");

Result:

Hello World

docs.title: Using Display
docs.text:
Any type implementing Display can be formatted.

struct Person {
    name: String;
}

extend Person : Display {
    func ToString(self) -> String {
        return self.name;
    }
}

let person = Person {
    name: String::From("Alex")
};

let text = Format("{}", person);

Result:

Alex

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Arguments are formatted by calling ToString().
  • Placeholders are processed from left to right.
  • Extra arguments are ignored.
  • Missing arguments produce no output for the corresponding placeholder.
  • A new string is always returned.

docs.title: Current Limitations
docs.text:
The current implementation:

  • Supports only the {} placeholder.
  • Does not support format specifiers such as width, alignment, precision, or numeric formatting.
  • Uses an internal fixed-size buffer of 1024 bytes.
  • Output exceeding the buffer size is truncated.

Example:

Format("Hello {}", name);

Supported.

Format("{:08}", value);

Not currently supported.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Display
  • String
  • StringBuilder

docs.title: String
docs.text:
A dynamically-sized UTF-8 byte string type.

Module: Std

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
struct String {
    data: *char8;
    length: uint;
}

String::New()
String::From(str: *const char8, length: uint)
String::From(slice: char8[])

Clone() -> String

operator +(other: String) -> String
operator +(slice: char8[]) -> String

ToString() -> String

Data() -> *char8
Length() -> uint
IsEmpty() -> bool8

ToUpper() -> String
ToLower() -> String
Capitalize() -> String
TitleCase() -> String
Trim() -> String

StartsWith(prefix: String) -> bool
EndsWith(suffix: String) -> bool

Split(delimiter: char8) -> StringArray

Repeat(count: uint) -> String

ToUpperInPlace()
ToLowerInPlace()
CapitalizeInPlace()
TrimInPlace()
TitleCaseInPlace()

docs.title: Description
docs.text:
String represents a sequence of bytes stored in dynamically allocated memory.

Strings are immutable by convention, although some methods provide in-place modification of the underlying data.

String implements the Display interface.

docs.title: String
docs.text:
struct String {
    data: *char8;
    length: uint;
}
Field Description data Pointer to the character buffer. length Number of bytes in the string.

docs.title: StringArray
docs.text:
struct StringArray {
    data: *String;
    length: uint;
}
Field Description data Pointer to the array of strings. length Number of strings in the array.

docs.title: New
docs.text:
String::New() -> String

Creates an empty string.

docs.title: Example
docs.text: let str = String::New();

docs.title: From Pointer
docs.text:
String::From(str: *const char8, length: uint) -> String

Creates a string by copying raw character data.

docs.title: Example
docs.text: let str = String::From(ptr, length);

docs.title: From Slice
docs.text:
String::From(slice: char8[]) -> String

Creates a string by copying a character slice.

docs.title: Example
docs.text: let str = String::From("Hello");

docs.title: Clone
docs.text:
Clone() -> String

Returns a copy of the string.

docs.title: Example
docs.text: let copy = original.Clone();

docs.title: String + String
docs.text:
stringA + stringB

Returns a new string containing both strings concatenated together.

docs.title: Example
docs.text: let result = hello + world;

docs.title: String + char8[ ]
docs.text:
string + slice

Returns a new string containing the string followed by the character slice.

docs.title: Example
docs.text: let result = str + " World";

docs.title: Data
docs.text:
Data() -> *char8

Returns a pointer to the underlying character buffer.

docs.title: Length
docs.text:
Length() -> uint

Returns the number of bytes stored in the string.

docs.title: IsEmpty
docs.text:
IsEmpty() -> bool8

Returns true if the string length is zero.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
if str.IsEmpty() {
    Print("empty");
}

docs.title: ToUpper
docs.text:
ToUpper() -> String

Returns a new uppercase string.

docs.title: Example
docs.text: let upper = str.ToUpper();

docs.title: ToLower
docs.text:
ToLower() -> String

Returns a new lowercase string.

docs.title: Capitalize
docs.text:
Capitalize() -> String

Returns a new string with the first character uppercase and the remaining characters lowercase.

docs.title: Example
docs.text: let result = str.Capitalize();

docs.title: TitleCase
docs.text:
TitleCase() -> String

Returns a new string with the first letter of each word capitalized.

docs.title: Example
docs.text: let result = str.TitleCase();

docs.title: Trim
docs.text:
Trim() -> String

Returns a copy of the string with leading and trailing whitespace removed.

Whitespace characters recognized:

  • Space (' ')
  • Tab ('\t')
  • Newline ('\n')
  • Carriage return ('\r')

docs.title: Example
docs.text: let result = str.Trim();

docs.title: StartsWith
docs.text:
StartsWith(prefix: String) -> bool

Returns true if the string begins with the specified prefix.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
if str.StartsWith(String::From("Hello")) {

}

docs.title: EndsWith
docs.text:
EndsWith(suffix: String) -> bool

Returns true if the string ends with the specified suffix.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
if str.EndsWith(String::From(".txt")) {
}

docs.title: Split
docs.text:
Split(delimiter: char8) -> StringArray

Splits the string into multiple substrings separated by the specified delimiter.

docs.title: Example
docs.text: let tokens = str.Split(',');

docs.title: Result
docs.text:
"a,b,c"

↓

["a", "b", "c"]

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• The returned strings reference the original string's data.
  • No character data is copied.
  • The original string must remain valid while the returned StringArray is in use.

docs.title: Repeat
docs.text:
Repeat(count: uint) -> String

Returns a new string containing the original string repeated count times.

Internally, Repeat uses StringBuilder to efficiently construct the resulting string, reducing unnecessary memory allocations and copies.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
let result = String::From("Hi").Repeat(3);

Result:

HiHiHi

Result:

HiHiHi

docs.title: In-Place Operations
docs.text: These functions modify the existing string buffer directly.

docs.title: ToUpperInPlace
docs.text:
ToUpperInPlace()

Converts all characters to uppercase.

docs.title: ToLowerInPlace
docs.text:
ToLowerInPlace()

Converts all characters to lowercase.

docs.title: CapitalizeInPlace
docs.text:
CapitalizeInPlace()

Capitalizes the first character and lowercases the remaining characters.

docs.title: TrimInPlace
docs.text:
TrimInPlace()

Removes leading and trailing whitespace from the existing buffer.

docs.title: TitleCaseInPlace
docs.text:
TitleCaseInPlace()

Capitalizes the first letter of each word directly in the existing buffer.

docs.title: ToString
docs.text:
ToString() -> String

Returns the string itself.

This method exists to satisfy the Display interface.

docs.title: Display Implementation
docs.text:
String implements the Display interface.

extend String : Display {
    func ToString(self) -> String {
        return *self;
    }
}

Calling ToString on a String returns the string itself. More info about the Display in Display.md

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
let str = String::From("Hello");

let text = str.ToString();

docs.title: StringBuilder
docs.text:
Efficiently constructs strings through incremental appends.

Module: Std

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
struct StringBuilder {
    data: *char8;
    length: uint;
    capacity: uint;
}

StringBuilder::New()
StringBuilder::WithCapacity(capacity: uint)

Capacity() -> uint
Length() -> uint
IsEmpty() -> bool8

Reserve(additional: uint)
Shrink()
Clear()

Append(c: char8)
Append(str: char8[])
Append(str: String)

ToString() -> String
IntoString() -> String

docs.title: Description
docs.text:
StringBuilder is a mutable string construction utility designed for efficiently building large strings.

Unlike repeatedly concatenating String values, which may require allocating and copying memory for every operation, StringBuilder maintains an expandable internal buffer and grows it as needed.

This makes it ideal for:

  • Generating text
  • Building formatted output
  • Constructing large strings
  • Repeated append operations

docs.title: StringBuilder
docs.text:
struct StringBuilder {
    data: *char8;
    length: uint;
    capacity: uint;
}
Field Description data Pointer to the internal character buffer. length Number of bytes currently stored. capacity Total allocated buffer size.

docs.title: New
docs.text:
StringBuilder::New() -> StringBuilder

Creates an empty string builder.

docs.title: Example
docs.text: let builder = StringBuilder::New();

docs.title: WithCapacity
docs.text:
StringBuilder::WithCapacity(capacity: uint) -> StringBuilder

Creates a string builder with a preallocated buffer.

Preallocating capacity can reduce memory reallocations when the final size is known.

docs.title: Example
docs.text: let builder = StringBuilder::WithCapacity(1024);

docs.title: Capacity
docs.text:
Capacity() -> uint

Returns the current buffer capacity.

docs.title: Example
docs.text: Print(Format("{}", builder.Capacity()));

docs.title: Reserve
docs.text:
Reserve(additional: uint)

Ensures sufficient capacity exists for additional bytes.

The buffer grows automatically when required.

docs.title: Example
docs.text: builder.Reserve(512);

docs.title: Shrink
docs.text:
Shrink()

Reduces the internal buffer size to exactly match the current length.

This can be used to release unused memory.

docs.title: Example
docs.text: builder.Shrink();

docs.title: Append Character
docs.text:
Append(c: char8)

Appends a single character.

docs.title: Example
docs.text: builder.Append('A');

docs.title: Append Slice
docs.text:
Append(str: char8[])

Appends a character slice.

docs.title: Example
docs.text: builder.Append("Hello");

docs.title: Append String
docs.text:
Append(str: String)

Appends a String.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
let name = String::From("Alex");

builder.Append(name);

docs.title: ToString
docs.text:
ToString() -> String

Creates a new String containing a copy of the builder contents.

The builder remains unchanged.

docs.title: Example
docs.text: let text = builder.ToString();

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Allocates new memory.
  • Copies all stored characters.
  • The builder can continue to be used afterwards.

docs.title: IntoString
docs.text:
IntoString() -> String

Transfers ownership of the internal buffer into a String.

After the conversion, the builder becomes empty.

docs.title: Example
docs.text: let text = builder.IntoString();

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Does not copy the character data.
  • More efficient than ToString().
  • Resets the builder to an empty state.

docs.title: Length
docs.text:
Length() -> uint

Returns the current number of bytes stored.

docs.title: Example
docs.text: let len = builder.Length();

docs.title: IsEmpty
docs.text:
IsEmpty() -> bool8

Returns true if the builder contains no characters.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
if builder.IsEmpty() {
    Print("empty");
}

docs.title: Clear
docs.text:
Clear()

Removes all contents from the builder.

The allocated buffer remains available for future use.

docs.title: Example
docs.text: builder.Clear();

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Does not free memory.
  • Sets the length to zero.
  • Existing capacity is preserved.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
import Std::StringBuilder;
import Std::Io::Print;

func Main() {
    let builder = StringBuilder::New();

    builder.Append("Hello");
    builder.Append(' ');
    builder.Append("World");

    let text = builder.IntoString();

    Print(text);
}

Output:

Hello World

docs.title: Capacity Growth
docs.text:
StringBuilder automatically grows its internal buffer when additional space is required.

The capacity is increased geometrically (typically doubled), reducing the number of memory allocations required during repeated append operations.

Example growth pattern:

16 → 32 → 64 → 128 → 256 → ...

This strategy provides efficient amortized append performance.

docs.title: Choosing Between ToString and IntoString
docs.text:
Method Copies Data Builder Remains Usable ToString() Yes Yes IntoString() No No

Use ToString() when the builder must continue being used.

Use IntoString() when the builder is no longer needed and maximum efficiency is desired.

docs.title: Boolean Types
docs.text:
Boolean types represent logical truth values.

Module: Std

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
ToString(value: bool8) -> String
ToString(value: bool16) -> String
ToString(value: bool32) -> String

bool8.ToString() -> String
bool16.ToString() -> String
bool32.ToString() -> String

docs.title: Description
docs.text:
Rux provides multiple boolean types with different storage sizes:

Type Size bool8 8 bits bool16 16 bits bool32 32 bits

All boolean types represent the same logical values:

true
false

The primary difference between them is storage size.

All boolean types implement the Display interface and can be used with formatting and output functions.

docs.title: bool8
docs.text: ToString(value: bool8) -> String

docs.title: bool16
docs.text: ToString(value: bool16) -> String

docs.title: bool32
docs.text:
ToString(value: bool32) -> String

Converts a boolean value into its string representation.

docs.title: Return Values
docs.text: Boolean Value Result true "true" false "false"

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
let text = ToString(true);

Result:

true

docs.title: Display Implementation
docs.text:
All boolean types implement the Display interface.

extend bool8 : Display
extend bool16 : Display
extend bool32 : Display

This allows boolean values to be used with APIs accepting Display.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
PrintLine(true);

Output:

true

docs.title: Formatting
docs.text: Boolean values can be used with Format().

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
let text = Format(
    "Enabled: {}",
    true
);

Result:

Enabled: true

docs.title: bool8
docs.text:
let value: bool8 = true;

PrintLine(value);

Output:

true

docs.title: bool16
docs.text:
let value: bool16 = false;

PrintLine(value);

Output:

false

docs.title: bool32
docs.text:
let value: bool32 = true;

PrintLine(value);

Output:

true

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• All boolean types behave identically.
  • The only difference between bool8, bool16, and bool32 is storage size.
  • String conversions always produce lowercase output.
  • A new string is allocated for each conversion.

docs.title: Character Types
docs.text:
Character types represent individual Unicode code points.

Module: Std

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
ToString(value: char8) -> String
ToString(value: char16) -> String
ToString(value: char32) -> String

char8.ToString() -> String
char16.ToString() -> String
char32.ToString() -> String

docs.title: Description
docs.text:
Rux provides multiple character types with different storage sizes:

Type Size Unicode Range char8 8 bits U+0000 - U+00FF char16 16 bits U+0000 - U+FFFF char32 32 bits Full Unicode range

All character types implement the Display interface.

String conversion functions encode characters as UTF-8 and return the result as a String.

docs.title: char8
docs.text:
ToString(value: char8) -> String

Converts a single-byte character into a String.

docs.title: char16
docs.text:
ToString(value: char16) -> String

Converts a UTF-16 code point into a UTF-8 encoded String.

docs.title: char32
docs.text:
ToString(value: char32) -> String

Converts a UTF-32 code point into a UTF-8 encoded String.

docs.title: Return Value
docs.text: Returns a UTF-8 encoded String containing the specified character.

docs.title: UTF-8 Encoding
docs.text:
The resulting string length depends on the Unicode code point.

Unicode Range UTF-8 Length U+0000 - U+007F 1 byte U+0080 - U+07FF 2 bytes U+0800 - U+FFFF 3 bytes U+10000 - U+10FFFF 4 bytes

docs.title: Display Implementation
docs.text:
All character types implement the Display interface.

extend char8 : Display
extend char16 : Display
extend char32 : Display

This allows characters to be used with formatting and output APIs.

docs.title: char8
docs.text:
let c: char8 = 'A';

PrintLine(c);

Output:

A

docs.title: char16
docs.text:
let c: char16 = 'Ω';

PrintLine(c);

Output:

Ω

docs.title: char32
docs.text:
let c: char32 = '😀';

PrintLine(c);

Output:

😀

docs.title: Formatting
docs.text: Character values can be used with Format().

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
let text = Format(
    "Character: {}",
    'A'
);

Result:

Character: A

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• All conversions return UTF-8 encoded strings.
  • char8 always produces a one-byte string.
  • char16 may produce a one-, two-, or three-byte UTF-8 sequence.
  • char32 may produce a one-, two-, three-, or four-byte UTF-8 sequence.
  • A new string is allocated for each conversion.

docs.title: char16
docs.text:
The current implementation encodes the character value directly as a Unicode code point.

UTF-16 surrogate pairs are not currently supported.

Characters outside the Basic Multilingual Plane (BMP) therefore require char32.

docs.title: Floating-Point Types
docs.text:
Floating-point types represent real numbers with fractional components.

Module: Std

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
ToString(value: float32) -> String
ToString(value: float64) -> String

float32.ToString() -> String
float64.ToString() -> String

IsNan(value: float64) -> bool
IsInfinite(value: float64) -> bool

docs.title: Description
docs.text:
Rux provides two floating-point types:

Type Size float32 32 bits float64 64 bits

Both types implement the Display interface and can be converted to strings using ToString().

float32 conversions are internally performed through float64.

docs.title: float32
docs.text:
ToString(value: float32) -> String

Converts a float32 value into a string.

Internally, the value is converted to float64 and formatted using the float64 implementation.

docs.title: float64
docs.text:
ToString(value: float64) -> String

Converts a float64 value into a string representation.

Special floating-point values are handled automatically:

Value Result NaN "NaN" Positive Infinity "Inf" Negative Infinity "-Inf"

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
let text = ToString(3.141592);

Result:

3.141592

docs.title: Display Implementation
docs.text:
Both floating-point types implement the Display interface.

extend float32 : Display
extend float64 : Display

This allows floating-point values to be used with formatting and output functions.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
PrintLine(3.14);

Output:

3.140000

docs.title: IsNan
docs.text:
IsNan(value: float64) -> bool

Returns true if the value is NaN (Not a Number).

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
if IsNan(value) {

}

docs.title: IsInfinite
docs.text:
IsInfinite(value: float64) -> bool

Returns true if the value is positive or negative infinity.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
if IsInfinite(value) {

}

docs.title: Fixed-Point Notation
docs.text:
Most values are formatted using fixed-point notation.

Example:

ToString(123.456);

Result:

123.456000

The current implementation outputs six digits after the decimal point.

docs.title: Scientific Notation
docs.text:
Very large and very small values are automatically formatted using scientific notation.

Example:

ToString(1234567890123456.0);

Possible result:

1.2345678901234567e+15

Example:

ToString(0.00001);

Possible result:

1.0000000000000000e-05

docs.title: float32
docs.text:
let value: float32 = 3.14f32;

PrintLine(value);

Output:

3.140000

docs.title: float64
docs.text:
let value: float64 = 123.456;

PrintLine(value);

Output:

123.456000

docs.title: NaN
docs.text:
PrintLine(ToString(0.0 / 0.0));

Output:

NaN

docs.title: Infinity
docs.text:
PrintLine(ToString(1.0 / 0.0));

Output:

Inf

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• float32 uses the float64 formatter internally.
  • A new string is allocated for every conversion.
  • Fixed-point formatting currently outputs six fractional digits.
  • Scientific notation is used for extremely large or small values.
  • Special values (NaN, Inf, -Inf) are supported.

docs.title: Current Limitations
docs.text:
• Formatting precision is currently fixed.
  • Rounding control is not currently available.
  • Custom formatting options are not currently supported.
  • Scientific notation thresholds are implementation-defined.

Future versions may provide configurable floating-point formatting.

docs.title: Integer Types
docs.text:
Signed integer types represent whole numbers that may be positive, negative, or zero.

Module: Std

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
ToString(value: int8) -> String
ToString(value: int16) -> String
ToString(value: int32) -> String
ToString(value: int64) -> String

int8.ToString() -> String
int16.ToString() -> String
int32.ToString() -> String
int64.ToString() -> String

docs.title: Description
docs.text:
Rux provides multiple signed integer types:

Type Size Range int8 8 bits -128 to 127 int16 16 bits -32,768 to 32,767 int32 32 bits -2,147,483,648 to 2,147,483,647 int64 64 bits -9,223,372,036,854,775,808 to 9,223,372,036,854,775,807

All integer types implement the Display interface.

docs.title: int8
docs.text:
ToString(value: int8) -> String

Converts an int8 value into its decimal string representation.

Internally, the value is converted to int64 and formatted using the int64 implementation.

docs.title: int16
docs.text:
ToString(value: int16) -> String

Converts an int16 value into its decimal string representation.

Internally, the value is converted to int64 and formatted using the int64 implementation.

docs.title: int32
docs.text:
ToString(value: int32) -> String

Converts an int32 value into its decimal string representation.

Internally, the value is converted to int64 and formatted using the int64 implementation.

docs.title: int64
docs.text:
ToString(value: int64) -> String

Converts an int64 value into its decimal string representation.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
ToString(42);

Result:

42

ToString(-123);

Result:

-123

ToString(0);

Result:

0

docs.title: Display Implementation
docs.text:
All signed integer types implement the Display interface.

extend int8  : Display
extend int16 : Display
extend int32 : Display
extend int64 : Display

This allows integer values to be used with formatting and output functions.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
PrintLine(42);

Output:

42

docs.title: Formatting
docs.text: Integer values can be used with Format().

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
let score: int32 = 9001;

let text = Format(
    "Score: {}",
    score
);

Result:

Score: 9001

docs.title: int8
docs.text:
let value: int8 = -10;

PrintLine(value);

Output:

-10

docs.title: int16
docs.text:
let value: int16 = 2048;

PrintLine(value);

Output:

2048

docs.title: int32
docs.text:
let value: int32 = -123456;

PrintLine(value);

Output:

-123456

docs.title: int64
docs.text:
let value: int64 = 9223372036854775807;

PrintLine(value);

Output:

9223372036854775807

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• All conversions produce decimal output.
  • Negative numbers are prefixed with -.
  • Zero is represented as "0".
  • int8, int16, and int32 use the int64 formatter internally.
  • A new string is allocated for each conversion.

docs.title: Current Limitations
docs.text:
• Only decimal formatting is currently supported.
  • Hexadecimal, binary, and octal formatting are not currently available.
  • Width, padding, alignment, and sign formatting options are not currently supported.

Future versions may add additional integer formatting capabilities.

docs.title: Unsigned Integer Types
docs.text:
Unsigned integer types represent non-negative whole numbers.

Module: Std

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
ToString(value: uint8) -> String
ToString(value: uint16) -> String
ToString(value: uint32) -> String
ToString(value: uint64) -> String

uint8.ToString() -> String
uint16.ToString() -> String
uint32.ToString() -> String
uint64.ToString() -> String

docs.title: Description
docs.text:
Rux provides multiple unsigned integer types:

Type Size Range uint8 8 bits 0 to 255 uint16 16 bits 0 to 65,535 uint32 32 bits 0 to 4,294,967,295 uint64 64 bits 0 to 18,446,744,073,709,551,615

All unsigned integer types implement the Display interface.

docs.title: uint8
docs.text:
ToString(value: uint8) -> String

Converts a uint8 value into its decimal string representation.

Internally, the value is converted to uint64 and formatted using the uint64 implementation.

docs.title: uint16
docs.text:
ToString(value: uint16) -> String

Converts a uint16 value into its decimal string representation.

Internally, the value is converted to uint64 and formatted using the uint64 implementation.

docs.title: uint32
docs.text:
ToString(value: uint32) -> String

Converts a uint32 value into its decimal string representation.

Internally, the value is converted to uint64 and formatted using the uint64 implementation.

docs.title: uint64
docs.text:
ToString(value: uint64) -> String

Converts a uint64 value into its decimal string representation.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
ToString(42u64);

Result:

42

ToString(0u64);

Result:

0

docs.title: Display Implementation
docs.text:
All unsigned integer types implement the Display interface.

extend uint8  : Display
extend uint16 : Display
extend uint32 : Display
extend uint64 : Display

This allows unsigned integer values to be used with formatting and output functions.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
PrintLine(255u8);

Output:

255

docs.title: Formatting
docs.text: Unsigned integer values can be used with Format().

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
let score: uint32 = 9001;

let text = Format(
    "Score: {}",
    score
);

Result:

Score: 9001

docs.title: uint8
docs.text:
let value: uint8 = 255;

PrintLine(value);

Output:

255

docs.title: uint16
docs.text:
let value: uint16 = 65535;

PrintLine(value);

Output:

65535

docs.title: uint32
docs.text:
let value: uint32 = 4294967295;

PrintLine(value);

Output:

4294967295

docs.title: uint64
docs.text:
let value: uint64 =
    18446744073709551615;

PrintLine(value);

Output:

18446744073709551615

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• All conversions produce decimal output.
  • Unsigned integers cannot represent negative values.
  • Zero is represented as "0".
  • uint8, uint16, and uint32 use the uint64 formatter internally.
  • A new string is allocated for each conversion.

docs.title: Current Limitations
docs.text:
• Only decimal formatting is currently supported.
  • Hexadecimal, binary, and octal formatting are not currently available.
  • Width, padding, alignment, and sign formatting options are not currently supported.

Future versions may add additional integer formatting capabilities.

docs.title: Math
docs.text: Module: Std::Math

docs.title: Random
docs.text:
Pseudo-random number generation utilities.

Module: Std::Random

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
RandomInt8(min: int8, max: int8, seed: uint64) -> int8
RandomInt16(min: int16, max: int16, seed: uint64) -> int16
RandomInt32(min: int32, max: int32, seed: uint64) -> int32
RandomInt64(min: int64, max: int64, seed: uint64) -> int64
RandomInt(min: int, max: int, seed: uint64) -> int

RandomUInt8(min: uint8, max: uint8, seed: uint64) -> uint8
RandomUInt16(min: uint16, max: uint16, seed: uint64) -> uint16
RandomUInt32(min: uint32, max: uint32, seed: uint64) -> uint32
RandomUInt64(min: uint64, max: uint64, seed: uint64) -> uint64
RandomUInt(min: uint, max: uint, seed: uint64) -> uint

RandomFloat32(min: float32, max: float32, seed: uint64) -> float32
RandomFloat64(min: float64, max: float64, seed: uint64) -> float64

RandomBool(seed: uint64) -> bool

docs.title: Description
docs.text:
The Std::Random module provides deterministic pseudo-random number generation functions for integer, floating-point, and boolean values.

All functions require an explicit seed value.

Using the same seed will always produce the same result.

docs.title: RandomInt8
docs.text:
RandomInt8(min: int8, max: int8, seed: uint64) -> int8

Returns a pseudo-random int8 value in the range [min, max)

docs.title: RandomInt16
docs.text:
RandomInt16(min: int16, max: int16, seed: uint64) -> int16

Returns a pseudo-random int16 value in the range [min, max)

docs.title: RandomInt32
docs.text:
RandomInt32(min: int32, max: int32, seed: uint64) -> int32

Returns a pseudo-random int32 value in the range [min, max)

docs.title: RandomInt64
docs.text:
RandomInt64(min: int64, max: int64, seed: uint64) -> int64

Returns a pseudo-random int64 value in the range [min, max)

docs.title: RandomInt
docs.text:
RandomInt(min: int, max: int, seed: uint64) -> int

Returns a pseudo-random platform-sized integer in the range:

[min, max)

docs.title: RandomUInt8
docs.text:
RandomUInt8(min: uint8, max: uint8, seed: uint64) -> uint8

Returns a pseudo-random uint8 value in the range [min, max)

docs.title: RandomUInt16
docs.text:
RandomUInt16(min: uint16, max: uint16, seed: uint64) -> uint16

Returns a pseudo-random uint16 value in the range [min, max)

docs.title: RandomUInt32
docs.text:
RandomUInt32(min: uint32, max: uint32, seed: uint64) -> uint32

Returns a pseudo-random uint32 value in the range [min, max)

docs.title: RandomUInt64
docs.text:
RandomUInt64(min: uint64, max: uint64, seed: uint64) -> uint64

Returns a pseudo-random uint64 value in the range [min, max)

docs.title: RandomUInt
docs.text:
RandomUInt(min: uint, max: uint, seed: uint64) -> uint

Returns a pseudo-random platform-sized unsigned integer in the range:

[min, max)

docs.title: RandomFloat32
docs.text:
RandomFloat32(min: float32, max: float32, seed: uint64) -> float32

Returns a pseudo-random float32 value between min and max.

docs.title: Example
docs.text: let value = RandomFloat32(0.0f32, 1.0f32, seed);

docs.title: RandomFloat64
docs.text:
RandomFloat64(min: float64, max: float64, seed: uint64) -> float64

Returns a pseudo-random float64 value between min and max.

docs.title: Example
docs.text: let value = RandomFloat64(0.0, 1.0, seed);

docs.title: RandomBool
docs.text:
RandomBool(seed: uint64) -> bool

Returns either true or false.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
if RandomBool(seed) {
    Print("Heads");
} else {
    Print("Tails");
}

docs.title: Random Integer
docs.text:
import Std::Random;

let value = RandomInt(1, 101, 12345);

Possible result:

42

docs.title: Random Floating-Point Number
docs.text:
import Std::Random;

let value = RandomFloat64(
    0.0,
    100.0,
    12345
);

Possible result:

73.28

docs.title: Random Boolean
docs.text:
let result = RandomBool(12345);

Possible result:

true

docs.title: Errors
docs.text:
All range-based functions require:

min < max

If min >= max, the function terminates the program with:

Fatal("min must be less than max")

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• All functions are deterministic.
  • The same seed always produces the same result.
  • Different seeds generally produce different results.
  • Integer functions generate values in the range [min, max).
  • Floating-point functions generate values between min and max.
  • RandomBool() uses the least significant bit of the generated value.
  • RandomSeed() is planned for as soon as 0.1.7 to make the library more convinient to use

docs.title: Implementation Notes
docs.text:
The module currently uses an internal Xorshift-style pseudo-random number generator.

This generator is:

  • Fast
  • Deterministic
  • Suitable for simulations, testing, and games

It is not cryptographically secure and should not be used for:

  • Password generation
  • Cryptographic keys
  • Security-sensitive randomness
  • Authentication tokens

For security-sensitive applications, use a cryptographically secure random source instead.

docs.title: Time
docs.text:
Time-related utilities for sleeping, measuring elapsed time, and retrieving system time.

Module: Std::Time

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
struct SystemTime

SleepMs(ms: uint32)
SleepSeconds(seconds: uint32)
SleepMinutes(minutes: uint32)

TickMs() -> uint64

LocalTime() -> SystemTime
UtcTime() -> SystemTime

docs.title: Description
docs.text:
The Std::Time module provides basic timing functionality:

  • Sleeping for a specified duration
  • Retrieving monotonic tick counts
  • Accessing local and UTC system time
  • Inspecting date and time components

The implementation is platform-specific where required.

docs.title: SystemTime
docs.text:
Represents a calendar date and time.

struct SystemTime {
    year: uint16;
    month: uint16;
    dayOfWeek: uint16;
    day: uint16;
    hour: uint16;
    minute: uint16;
    second: uint16;
    milliseconds: uint16;
}
Field Description year Year component. month Month (1-12). dayOfWeek Day of week. day Day of month. hour Hour (0-23). minute Minute (0-59). second Second (0-59). milliseconds Millisecond component.

docs.title: SleepMs
docs.text:
SleepMs(ms: uint32)

Suspends execution for approximately the specified number of milliseconds.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
SleepMs(500);

Pauses execution for roughly half a second.

docs.title: SleepSeconds
docs.text:
SleepSeconds(seconds: uint32)

Suspends execution for approximately the specified number of seconds.

docs.title: Example
docs.text: SleepSeconds(2);

docs.title: SleepMinutes
docs.text:
SleepMinutes(minutes: uint32)

Suspends execution for approximately the specified number of minutes.

docs.title: Example
docs.text: SleepMinutes(1);

docs.title: TickMs
docs.text:
TickMs() -> uint64

Returns a monotonic millisecond tick count.

This value is intended for measuring elapsed time and should not be interpreted as a calendar date or wall-clock timestamp.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
let start = TickMs();

SleepMs(250);

let elapsed = TickMs() - start;

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Monotonic clocks are not affected by system clock adjustments.
  • Useful for benchmarking and measuring durations.
  • The exact implementation is platform-specific.

docs.title: LocalTime
docs.text:
LocalTime() -> SystemTime

Returns the current local system time.

docs.title: Example
docs.text: let time = LocalTime();

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• On Windows, local time is retrieved from the operating system.
  • On Linux, the current implementation returns the same value as UtcTime().

docs.title: UtcTime
docs.text:
UtcTime() -> SystemTime

Returns the current Coordinated Universal Time (UTC).

docs.title: Example
docs.text: let time = UtcTime();

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
import Std::Time;

func Main() {

    let start = TickMs();

    SleepSeconds(1);

    let elapsed = TickMs() - start;

    let utc = UtcTime();

    Print(Format(
        "Elapsed: {} ms\nUTC: {}-{}-{} {}:{}:{}",
        elapsed,
        utc.year,
        utc.month,
        utc.day,
        utc.hour,
        utc.minute,
        utc.second
    ));
}

docs.title: Windows
docs.text:
• SleepMs() uses the Windows sleep API.
  • TickMs() uses a monotonic tick counter.
  • LocalTime() returns the system's local time.
  • UtcTime() returns UTC from the operating system.

docs.title: Linux
docs.text:
• SleepMs() uses nanosleep.
  • TickMs() uses clock_gettime(CLOCK_MONOTONIC).
  • UtcTime() is computed from CLOCK_REALTIME.
  • LocalTime() currently returns UTC.

docs.title: Day Of Week
docs.text:
The meaning of dayOfWeek is platform-dependent.

Applications should avoid assuming a specific mapping unless documented by the target platform.

Common mappings include:

Value Day 0 Sunday 1 Monday 2 Tuesday 3 Wednesday 4 Thursday 5 Friday 6 Saturday

The exact mapping should not be relied upon without platform verification.
